flashcards.front: What is a page replacement algorithm?
flashcards.back: A technique used by OS to decide which memory page to replace when a new page needs to be loaded but no free frames are available.
An interrupt that occurs when a program accesses a memory page that is not currently in RAM, requiring the OS to load it from secondary storage.
First In First Out - replaces the oldest page in memory (the one that has been in memory the longest time).
Replaces the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future - achieves minimum page faults but is impractical to implement.
Least Recently Used - replaces the page that has not been used for the longest time in the recent past.
Least Frequently Used - replaces the page with the lowest reference count/frequency of access.
The phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames can lead to an increase in page faults (contrary to expectation). FIFO exhibits this anomaly.

flashcards.front: What is the Second Chance (Clock) algorithm?
flashcards.back: A modified FIFO that gives pages a second chance if their reference bit is set; uses a circular buffer and clock hand.

flashcards.front: What is the reference bit in page replacement?
flashcards.back: A bit associated with each page that indicates whether the page has been recently accessed (1) or not (0).
Excessive page swapping between memory and disk due to too many page faults, causing severe degradation in system performance.
The set of pages that a process has referenced during the most recent τ (tau) time interval.

flashcards.front: What is a frame in memory management?
flashcards.back: A fixed-size block of physical memory that holds one page of virtual memory.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between global and local page replacement?
flashcards.back: Global: any page in memory can be replaced. Local: only pages belonging to the same process can be replaced.

flashcards.front: What is the access bit (dirty bit) in page tables?
flashcards.back: A bit indicating whether a page has been modified (written to). If set, the page must be written back to disk before eviction.

flashcards.front: Why is Optimal algorithm not practical to implement?
flashcards.back: It requires future knowledge of page references, which is impossible in real systems; it's only used for benchmarking.
